title: Scale drawings
description: Find a missing side length in a scale drawing when given either a set of corresponding side lengths or one side length and the scale factor.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a map shows that $3$ centimeters represents $10$ kilometers.

**What number of centimeters on the map represents an actual distance of $25$ kilometers?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] centimeters
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{3}\text{ cm}:\greenD{10}\text{ km}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{3}:\greenD{10}$ and the actual distance of $\greenD{25}\text{ km}$ to find the number of centimeters.

Centimeters | Kilometers
:-:|:-:
$3$ | $10$
$\downarrow \times 2.5$ | $\downarrow \times 2.5$
${?}$ | ${25}$

If the kilometers are $2.5$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the centimeters by $2.5$.
$\blueD3\times 2.5=7.5$
The number of centimeters on the map that represents an actual distance of $25$ kilometers is $7.5$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a map shows that $2.5$ centimeters represents $15$ kilometers.

**What number of actual kilometers are represented by $17.5$ centimeters on the map?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] kilometers
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{2.5}\text{ cm}:\greenD{15}\text{ km}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{2.5}:\greenD{15}$ and the  distance of $\blueD{17.5}\text{ cm}$ on the map to find the actual number  of kilometers.

Centimeters | Kilometers
:-:|:-:
$2.5$ | $15$
$\downarrow \times 7$ | $\downarrow \times 7$
${17.5}$ | ${?}$

If the centimeters are $7$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the kilometers by $7$.
$\greenD{15}\times 7=105$
The number of actual kilometers represented by $17.5$ centimeters on the map is $105$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a blue print drawing of a house shows that $6$ centimeters represents $3$ meters.

**What number of centimeters on the blue print represents an actual distance of $27$ meters?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] centimeters
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{6}\text{ cm}:\greenD{3}\text{ m}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{6}:\greenD{3}$ and the actual distance of $\greenD{27}\text{ m}$ to find the number of centimeters.

Centimeters | Meters
:-:|:-:
$6$ | $3$
$\downarrow \times 9$ | $\downarrow \times 9$
${?}$ | ${27}$

If the meters are $9$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the centimeters by $9$.
$\blueD6\times 9=54$
The number of centimeters on the blue print that represent an actual distance of $27$ meters is $54$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a blue print drawing of a house shows that $10$ centimeters represents $2$ meters.

**What number of  actual meters are represented by $18$ centimeters on the blue print?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] meters
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{10}\text{ cm}:\greenD{2}\text{ m}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{10}:\greenD{2}$ and the distance of $\blueD{18}\text{ cm}$ on the blueprint to find the number of actual meters.

Centimeters | Meters
:-:|:-:
$10$ | $2$
$\downarrow \times 1.8$ | $\downarrow \times 1.8$
${18}$ | ${?}$

If the centimeters are $1.8$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the meters by $1.8$.
$\blueD2\times 1.8=3.6$
The number of  actual meters that are represented by $18$ centimeters on the blue print is $3.6$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a hiking map shows that $1.5$ inches  represents $4$ miles.

**What number of  actual miles are represented by $6$ inches on the map?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] miles
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{1.5}\text{ inches}:\greenD{4}\text{ miles}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{1.5}:\greenD{4}$ and the  distance of $\blueD{6}\text{ inches}$ on the map to find the number of miles.

Inches | Miles
:-:|:-:
$1.5$ | $4$
$\downarrow \times 4$ | $\downarrow \times 4$
${6}$ | ${?}$

If the inches are $4$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the miles  by $4$.
$\greenD4\times 4=16$
The number of  actual miles that are represented by $6$ inches on the hiking map is $16$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A scale on a hiking map shows that $3$ inches represents $1.25$ miles.

**What number of  inches on the map represent $10$ actual miles?**

[[‚ėÉ input-number 3]] inches
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{3}\text{ inches}:\greenD{1.25}\text{ miles}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{3}:\greenD{1.25}$ and the  actual distance of $\greenD{10}\text{ miles}$ to find the number of inches on the map.

Inches | Miles
:-:|:-:
$3$ | $1.25$
$\downarrow \times 8$ | $\downarrow \times 8$
${?}$ | ${10}$

If the miles are $8$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the inches by $8$.
$\blueD3\times 8=24$
The number of  inches on the map that  represent $10$ actual miles is $24$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gwendolyn draws a hotdog on graph paper using the scale shown below. The hotdog has a length of $9$ units in the drawing.

![A hotdog on graph paper. A 9-unit long line is labeled, "length." A 6-unit long scale is labeled, "10 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/79c89b49febfb203065d1372498f7c2dc2eb79e8)

**What is the length, in $\text{cm}$, of the actual hotdog?**

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 2]] $\text{cm}$
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{6}\text{ units}:\greenD{10}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{6}:\greenD{10}$ and the  drawing length of $\blueD{9}\text{ cm}$ to find the number of units on the drawing.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$6$ | $10$
$\downarrow \times 1.5$ | $\downarrow \times 1.5$
${9}$ | ${?}$

If the length in units is $1.5$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the length in centimeters  by $1.5$.
$\greenD{10} \times 1.5=15$
The hotdog has an actual length of $15\text{ cm}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jaylynn draws a hen on graph paper using the scale shown below. The hen has a height of $16$ units in the drawing.

![A hen on graph paper. A 16-unit long line is labeled, "height." A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "6 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/30f4fd69d7c20747f92c2d602ddef723881003fd)

**What is the height, in $\text{cm}$, of the actual hen?**

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{2}\text{ units}:\greenD{6}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{2}:\greenD{6}$ and the  drawing height of $\blueD{16}$ units to find the height of the real hen.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$2$ | $6$
$\downarrow \times 8$ | $\downarrow \times 8$
${16}$ | ${?}$

If the height in units is $8$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the height in centimeters  by $8$.
$\greenD{6} \times 8=48$
The hen has an actual height of $48\text{ cm}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Babatunde draws a drinking glass on graph paper using the scale shown below. The glass has a length of $8$ units in the drawing.

![A drinking glass on graph paper. An 8-unit long line is labeled, "height." A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "2.5 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/8f9b806b62063f2820df3b1017150dbe24a3f4fe)

**What is the height, in $\text{cm}$, of the actual glass?**

[[‚ėÉ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{2}\text{ units}:\greenD{2.5}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{2}:\greenD{2.5}$ and the  drawing height of $\blueD{8}$ units to find the height of the real glass.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$2$ | $2.5$
$\downarrow \times 4$ | $\downarrow \times 4$
${8}$ | ${?}$

If the height in units is $4$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the height in centimeters  by $4$.
$\greenD{2.5} \times 4=10$
The glass has an actual height of $10\text{ cm}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Seren draws a chick on graph paper using the scale shown below. The chick has a height of $5\,\text{cm}$ in the drawing.

**Which of the following drawings best represents the height of the chick at the scale Seren chose?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{1}\text{ unit}:\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{1}\text{ unit}:\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\text{ cm}$ and the  real height of $\greenD{5}\,\text{cm}$ to find the height of the chick in the drawing.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\downarrow \times 10$ | $\downarrow \times 10$
${?}$ | ${5}$

If the height in centimeters is $10$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the height in units  by $10$.
$\blueD{1} \times 10=10$

The drawing should represent the chick with a height of $10$ units.

![A chick on graph paper. A line labeled "10 units" shows the height of the chick. A 1-unit long scale is labeled, "half centimeter."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/ae36a82467e409450e61f4af38b27d599a1d373d)

choices.content: ![A chick on graph paper. A line labeled "10 units" shows the height of the chick. A 1-unit long scale is labeled, "half centimeter."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/9664b4f1331720ff7249966006eff406edd4f804)

![A chick on graph paper. A line labeled "2.5 units" shows the height of the chick. A 1-unit long scale is labeled, "half centimeter."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/de9f99b15a553d3f5c6ed34166d25dd9feecaa0e)

![A chick on graph paper. A line labeled "5 units" shows the height of the chick. A 1-unit long scale is labeled, "half centimeter."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/95e482d42d30f65e249014d96f40a861ea5bef1c)

assessment_items.question_content:
Leonardo draws a cookie on graph paper using the scale shown below. The cookie has a diameter of $12.5\,\text{cm}$ in the drawing.

**Which of the following drawings best represents the diameter of the cookie at the scale Leonardo chose?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: Our scale is $\blueD{2}\text{ units}:\greenD{5}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueD{2}\text{ units}:\greenD{5}\text{ cm}$ and the  real diameter of $\greenD{12.5}\,\text{cm}$ to find the diameter of the cookie in the drawing.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$2$ | $5$
$\downarrow \times 2.5$ | $\downarrow \times 2.5$
${?}$ | ${12.5}$

If the diameter in centimeters is $2.5$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the diameter in units  by $2.5$.
$\blueD{2} \times 2.5=5$

The drawing should represent the cookie with a diameter of $5$ units.

![A cookie on graph paper. A line labeled "5 units" shows the diameter of the cookie. A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "5 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/1200e6e17ac26f3871e8a2495facac9833ee614a)

choices.content: ![A cookie on graph paper. A line labeled "12.5 units" shows the diameter of the cookie. A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "5 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/5772b7e25ecbc83fb83b2c41f70182dae81f65dd)

![A cookie on graph paper. A line labeled "5 units" shows the diameter of the cookie. A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "5 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/4609407861f2649a5d09fcdcb06113e3d9240429)

![A cookie on graph paper. A line labeled "2.5 units" shows the diameter of the cookie. A 2-unit long scale is labeled, "5 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/2e00d056e6086c5e5b62132fdf53719af14a6cf1)

assessment_items.question_content:
H√ľseyin draws a fox on graph paper using the scale shown below. The fox has a height of $80\,\text{cm}$ in the drawing.

**Which of the following drawings best represents the height of the fox at the scale H√ľseyin chose?**

[[‚ėÉ radio 1]]
hints: Our scale is $\blueE{3}\text{ units}:\purpleE{15}\text{ cm}$.

We can use our scale $\blueE{3}\text{ units}:\purpleE{15}\text{ cm}$ and the  real height of $\purpleE{80}\,\text{cm}$ to find the height of the fox in the drawing.

Units | Centimeters
:-:|:-:
$3$ | $15$
$\downarrow \div 3$ | $\downarrow \div 3$
$1$ | $5$
$\downarrow \times 16$ | $\downarrow \times 16$
${16}$ | ${80}$

If the height in centimeters is $\dfrac{16}{3}$ times as long, then we also need to multiply the height in units  by $\dfrac{16}{3}$.
$\blueE{3} \times \dfrac{16}{3}=16$

The drawing should represent the fox with a height of $16$ units.

[[‚ėÉ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A fox on graph paper. A line labeled "16 units" shows the height of the fox. A 3-unit long scale is labeled, "15 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/38f08a85495c72fa8a5a7877ce96a1d224038196)

![A fox on graph paper. A line labeled "5 and 1-third units" shows the height of the fox. A 3-unit long scale is labeled, "15 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/bd3cf28df1a8605a4051f257366970ae6a299ee5)

![A fox on graph paper. A line labeled "12 units" shows the height of the fox. A 3-unit long scale is labeled, "15 centimeters."](web+graphie:${‚ė£ LOCALPATH}/images/1ca11c806fc4f5ce9402784c3dedba02858a9bf4)
